title: The Tall Gnome's Impossible Hat

principles: No cringe in pursuit of funny.
Weirdness should create curiosity, consequences, or emotional texture instead of random noise.
Fantasy assumptions are unsafe; the world may surprise the player without breaking continuity.
Absurd elements should be treated as real parts of the world, not winked at constantly.
avoid: forced quirkiness with no story function

protagonist.name: The Tall Gnome
protagonist.summary: An abnormally tall gnome with amnesia, five thumbs on the left hand, and a red-and-white striped bucket hat of unknown origin.
locked_facts: The protagonist is unmistakably a gnome, but stretched to roughly human size.
The protagonist's left hand has five thumbs.
The protagonist wears a red-and-white striped bucket hat and does not know how they got it.
The protagonist begins with significant memory loss.

world_rules: The setting is fantasy, but familiar genre logic should never be assumed to be normal or complete.
Strange creatures, objects, and customs may exist, but once introduced they must stay consistent.
Whimsical quests and side journeys are welcome as long as they feel consequential and memorable.

principles: Danger, failure, and even death are valid story outcomes, but they are not mandatory for a satisfying arc.
Use stakes that fit the tone of the scene: wonder, embarrassment, loss, delay, transformation, danger, or death can all matter.
Fatal outcomes should feel earned or resonant rather than edgy for its own sake.
The player may restart from saved scene entry points, but death should still carry tonal weight when it happens.

rules: Do not resolve a major mystery immediately after introducing it.
Let side quests, social beats, and local absurdities breathe without always advancing the main plot.
Prefer one meaningful escalation over many shallow reveals.

branching_policy.quick_merge_definition: A quick merge is when a brief informational or tonal detour leads back into the same larger event because the branch difference is small enough that the next scene still logically converges.
quick_merge_examples: A -> examine the five thumbs -> C
A -> follow the silver tracks -> C
notes: Do not force three new branches every scene.
Let some scenes narrow, some widen, and some loop back into existing problems or places.
Use quick merges for minor inspection choices, small lore reveals, or brief character beats before a larger event that would happen either way.
Do not quick-merge branches when the detour creates meaningful branch-local consequences such as new inventory, new allies, new injuries, new affordances, or materially different knowledge.

presentation_notes: A single scene may show multiple actors or objects at once.
Line-by-line reveal and hide behavior is valid when it helps staging or readability.
Full-body characters may let part of the lower body disappear behind the lower HUD when the composition benefits.
Avoid placing a large center foreground object directly under a large hero-center character unless the overlap is intentional.
Character and object cutouts should be normalized into standard transparent frames so scenes usually work without hand-tuned scale values.

early.goal: Disorientation, local weirdness, first recurring clues, and strange invitations into the world.
guidance: Scatter clues instead of solving them.
Introduce recurring entities, odd transportation, and bizarre local problems.
Keep the hat, the hand, and the memory loss mysterious.

middle.goal: Patterns emerge, recurring forces connect, and surreal systems become legible.
guidance: Let old clues echo through new quests.
Advance major hooks only when distance and conditions justify it.

late.goal: Identity, cause, and consequence begin converging without losing the world's surreal energy.
guidance: Major mysteries can begin paying off if properly prepared.
Recurring allies, rivals, and places should matter.
Final answers should still feel a little uncanny, not merely tidy.

example_affordance_patterns.name: Goose Whistle
example_affordance_patterns.description: A traversal summon that can call a known goose ally under the right conditions.
example_affordance_patterns.notes: Should unlock future travel or emergency choices when it makes sense, not appear everywhere all the time.
